messages.content:
There is not much that can be done to make this function faster using window.onload or similar methods, as the primary bottleneck is the rendering process that's happening within the function itself. However, you can improve the performance of the rendering by using React.memo and optimizing the components being rendered. 

You can also try lazy loading components using React.lazy and Suspense to only load components when they are needed, and use code splitting to split your code into smaller chunks that can be loaded on demand. Additionally, you can try optimizing your use of CSS and images to reduce the overall size of the page. 

Overall, improving the performance of this function involves a combination of techniques, including optimizing the rendering process, lazy loading components, code splitting, and optimizing CSS and images.

The delay of 2 seconds might be caused by the rendering process itself and not by the `window.onload` event. One way to improve the performance is to use React's `React.memo` and `useMemo` to memoize components and avoid unnecessary re-rendering. 

Another technique to consider is code splitting. You can split your code into smaller chunks and load them on demand using React.lazy and Suspense. This can improve the initial load time and reduce the overall size of the page.

Also, you can optimize your CSS and images to reduce the size of the page. Additionally, you can use a CDN to deliver static assets such as images, CSS, and JavaScript files.

Overall, improving the performance of this function involves optimizing the rendering process, code splitting, optimizing CSS and images, and using a CDN to deliver static assets.
